content.text: Prompt Sunset Notification and Migration Process

A prompt sunset is the scheduled retirement of a prompt version, a prompt pattern, or an integration contract that the product team has decided to discontinue. In Claude integrations, sunsets arise most commonly from three sources: a Claude model version that Anthropic is retiring (which may change response format or capability in ways that require prompt updates), an internal prompt version that was replaced by a newer, higher-quality version that the old callers have not yet migrated to, and an architectural pattern embedded in prompts (such as a specific JSON schema or a particular multi-step chaining approach) that the team has replaced with a better approach.

Without a formal sunset process, deprecated prompt versions accumulate as silent technical debt. Callers continue using them because they appear to work — until the underlying model or service changes enough that the old prompt starts producing degraded output, at which point the regression is difficult to diagnose and the migration is urgent rather than planned. A structured sunset process converts that reactive scenario into a proactive one: callers receive enough lead time to migrate safely, the migration path is documented before the sunset date is set, and the team has objective data on who has migrated and who has not before the retirement date arrives.

attrs.alt: Prompt sunset lifecycle: T-90d announce, T-60d warning headers, T-30d block new adopters, T-7d final callout, T+0 retire with three migration tracks

content.text: The lifecycle above divides the sunset period into five milestones. The 90-day window is the recommended minimum for production Claude integrations; it can be extended to 180 days when the migration track is Architecture Migration, which requires redesigning multi-step prompt chains rather than simply swapping a version pin. The three migration tracks — Drop-in Replacement, Prompt Adaptation, and Architecture Migration — reflect the actual complexity of the change required, not just the team's preference. Classifying the track correctly at announcement time sets realistic expectations and prevents teams from under-allocating migration effort.
Sunset Notice Data Model

The first artefact in any sunset process is the sunset notice: a structured document that captures all the information a consuming team needs to plan and execute their migration. The notice is published at T-90 and kept current until the sunset date. It is the single authoritative source of truth for the sunset — migration guides, tracking dashboards, and notification emails all link to it rather than duplicating its content.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import List, Optional
import datetime


class MigrationTrack(Enum):
    DROP_IN_REPLACEMENT   = "drop_in"        # Version swap only, no prompt changes
    PROMPT_ADAPTATION     = "prompt_adapt"   # Prompt edits required
    ARCHITECTURE_MIGRATION = "arch_migrate"  # Multi-step redesign required


class SunsetStatus(Enum):
    DRAFT     = "draft"
    ACTIVE    = "active"     # Notice published; migration period open
    EXTENDED  = "extended"   # Sunset date pushed back
    RETIRED   = "retired"    # Prompt version is gone


@dataclass
class SunsetNotice:
    notice_id: str
    prompt_version_id: str       # e.g. "summarise-v2" or "claude-3-opus-20240229"
    sunset_date: str             # ISO-8601
    replacement_version_id: str  # What callers should migrate to
    migration_track: MigrationTrack
    migration_guide_url: str
    announced_date: str          # ISO-8601
    status: SunsetStatus = SunsetStatus.ACTIVE
    extension_reason: Optional[str] = None
    original_sunset_date: Optional[str] = None  # Set when extended

    def days_until_sunset(self) -> int:
        sunset = datetime.date.fromisoformat(self.sunset_date)
        return (sunset - datetime.date.today()).days

    def migration_window_days(self) -> int:
        announced = datetime.date.fromisoformat(self.announced_date)
        sunset = datetime.date.fromisoformat(self.sunset_date)
        return (sunset - announced).days

    def is_in_warning_period(self) -> bool:
        """True when within 60 days of sunset — warning headers should be active."""
        return 0 < self.days_until_sunset() <= 60

    def is_past_new_adopter_block(self) -> bool:
        """True when within 30 days of sunset — new callers must not adopt old version."""
        return 0 < self.days_until_sunset() <= 30

    def notice_summary(self) -> str:
        days = self.days_until_sunset()
        urgency = "URGENT" if days <= 30 else ("WARNING" if days <= 60 else "INFO")
        return (
            f"[{urgency}] {self.notice_id}: '{self.prompt_version_id}' "
            f"retires {self.sunset_date} ({days}d). "
            f"Migrate to '{self.replacement_version_id}' "
            f"via {self.migration_track.value} track."
        )
Caller Tracking and Migration Progress

Publishing a sunset notice without tracking whether callers have migrated is ineffective: without objective data on who is still using the deprecated version, the team cannot determine whether it is safe to retire it on the planned date or whether extensions are needed. The tracking model below records every known caller of the deprecated prompt version and updates their migration status as they complete each stage.
Migration Guide Structure

Every sunset notice links to a migration guide — a document that tells callers exactly what they need to change and how to validate that the change is correct. The structure of the migration guide is calibrated to the migration track: Drop-in Replacement guides are short (the change is a version pin swap), Prompt Adaptation guides detail each prompt change required, and Architecture Migration guides are full design documents. The guide must be published at T-90 alongside the sunset notice, not deferred until the warning period.
The migration guide for a Prompt Adaptation track typically follows this structure:
Section
Contents
Required?
What changed and why
Plain-language explanation of the change; what the new version does differently
Yes
Side-by-side diff
Old prompt vs new prompt, highlighting every changed line
Yes
Known behaviour differences
Cases where the new version produces observably different output that callers must handle
Yes
Eval test set
30–50 golden cases callers can run to verify their migration is correct
Yes
Canary deployment instructions
Step-by-step guide to routing 5% of traffic to new version and monitoring metrics
Yes
Rollback instructions
How to revert to the old version if canary metrics degrade
Yes
Exception request link
Process for teams that cannot meet the sunset date
Yes
Automating Sunset Enforcement

Manual enforcement of sunset dates is unreliable: it depends on someone remembering to check whether all callers have migrated before retiring the deprecated version, and it creates incentives for silent non-compliance (a caller that quietly continues using the old version after the sunset date may go undetected until a production incident reveals it). Automated enforcement removes the manual dependency and makes non-compliance immediately visible.
Exception and Extension Process

Some callers will legitimately be unable to migrate by the sunset date. A formal exception process is preferable to informally extending the sunset deadline: it requires the caller to commit to a revised migration date, documents the business reason for the extension, and prevents indefinite deferrals. Exceptions should require approval at the tech-lead level or above and should not be granted for more than one additional sprint unless the migration track is Architecture Migration.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional
import datetime


class ExceptionOutcome(Enum):
    APPROVED = "approved"
    DENIED   = "denied"
    PENDING  = "pending"


@dataclass
class SunsetExceptionRequest:
    request_id: str
    notice_id: str
    caller_id: str
    team_name: str
    requested_extension_date: str   # ISO-8601 revised sunset date
    business_reason: str
    migration_plan_summary: str     # What the team will do in the extension period
    requested_by: str
    outcome: ExceptionOutcome = ExceptionOutcome.PENDING
    approved_extension_date: Optional[str] = None
    approver: Optional[str] = None
    denial_reason: Optional[str] = None

    def extension_days_requested(self, original_sunset_date: str) -> int:
        original = datetime.date.fromisoformat(original_sunset_date)
        requested = datetime.date.fromisoformat(self.requested_extension_date)
        return (requested - original).days

    def is_reasonable_extension(self, original_sunset_date: str) -> bool:
        """Heuristic: extensions beyond 60 days need VP-level approval."""
        return self.extension_days_requested(original_sunset_date) <= 60

    def exception_summary(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"Exception {self.request_id} for notice {self.notice_id}:\n"
            f"  Caller: {self.caller_id} ({self.team_name})\n"
            f"  Requested extension: {self.requested_extension_date}\n"
            f"  Outcome: {self.outcome.value}\n"
            + (f"  Approved until: {self.approved_extension_date}" if self.approved_extension_date else "")
            + (f"  Denied: {self.denial_reason}" if self.denial_reason else "")
        )
Summary

A prompt sunset process converts the reactive experience of discovering that a deprecated prompt version is still in production on the retirement date into a proactive one where every caller has a documented migration path, adequate lead time, and objective progress tracking. The key artefacts are the sunset notice (published at T-90), the migration guide (linked from the notice, covering all three migration tracks), the caller tracking dashboard (updated continuously through the migration period), and the exception request process (for teams with legitimate constraints).
Publish the sunset notice and migration guide simultaneously at T-90; a notice without a guide gives callers nowhere to start.
Classify the migration track (Drop-in, Prompt Adaptation, Architecture Migration) at notice time — the track determines the realistic migration window and whether the 90-day minimum is sufficient.
Use weighted traffic completion rate as the primary sunset readiness metric, not raw caller count; a 95% caller count completion rate is irrelevant if the 5% remaining represents 60% of traffic.
Add API response headers (Deprecation, Sunset) at T-60 so callers see the warning in their logs even if they did not read the email notification.
Require all exception requests to include a revised migration date and a written plan; exceptions without a plan become permanent deferrals.
